title: Chapter 2 Quiz
description: Test your mastery over Swift's control flow, comparisons, logical operators, and switch statements!

questions.question: Which code block executes if an 'if' condition turns out to be false?
questions.explanation: The 'else' block serves as the 'Plan B' and executes when the primary if condition is false.

questions.question: Which operator checks if two values are EXACTLY equal?
questions.explanation: `==` is the equality operator. A single `=` is the assignment operator used to store data.

questions.question: Which operator means 'Not Equal' in Swift?
questions.explanation: The exclamation mark `!` mixed with an equal sign `!=` means 'Not Equal'.

questions.question: If you check `10 > 10`, Swift will return `true`.
questions.explanation: 10 is not greater than 10. They are equal. You need to use `>=` (Greater than or equal to) if you want it to be true.

questions.question: To use a logical AND operator, you type two _____ symbols.
questions.explanation: Logical AND uses the double ampersand `&&` symbol.

questions.question: In a Logical AND (`&&`) operation, how many sides must be true for the whole thing to be true?
questions.explanation: For an AND (`&&`) statement to pass, literally every combined condition must evaluate to true.

questions.question: To use a logical OR operator, you type two vertical _____ symbols.
questions.explanation: Logical OR uses `||`.

questions.question: What is the result of `false || true`?
questions.explanation: With an OR (`||`) operation, you only need ONE side to be true for the result to be true.

questions.question: Placing a `!` in front of a boolean value does what?
options: Reverses its value (e.g. true becomes false)
questions.explanation: The Logical NOT `!` operator flips the truthfulness of a statement.

questions.question: What happens if you have an `else if` chain that is 50 lines long?
options: You should probably replace it with a switch statement.
questions.explanation: Switch statements are designed specifically to be a cleaner, safer alternative to long and messy `else if` chains.

questions.question: In a switch statement, the `default:` block achieves what purpose?
options: It is the fallback if no other 'case' matched.
It is completely optional and useless.
questions.explanation: The `default` case is executed when all preceding cases failed to match the tested value.

questions.question: In Swift, Switch statements MUST be exhaustive. They must cover every single possibility.
questions.explanation: Swift enforces exhaustiveness. If a switch doesn't cover all possible cases, it won't compile unless you add a `default` case.

questions.question: How do you evaluate multiple values inside a SINGLE switch case?
questions.explanation: You can group multiple values using a comma. e.g., `case 1, 2, 3:`

questions.question: How do you create a closed range spanning from 10 to 20 inside a switch case?
questions.explanation: Swift closed ranges use 3 dots: `...`.

questions.question: Unlike older languages like C, Swift's switch cases do NOT automatically fall through to the next case.
questions.explanation: Swift statements are safe; once a case finishes, the switch exits immediately. (You have to use the explicit `fallthrough` keyword if you want C-like behavior).

questions.question: What will `print("apple" == "Apple")` output?
questions.explanation: Swift is case sensitive! A lowercase 'a' is not the same as an uppercase 'A', so the check is false.

questions.question: Which of these expressions results in true?
questions.explanation: `true && !false` becomes `true && true`, which equals true!

questions.question: If you combine `if` and `else`, you can write: `if condition { } _____ { }` to keep an alternative chain going.
questions.explanation: You can string them together with `else if`.

questions.question: Evaluate: `5 >= 5`
questions.explanation: 5 is equal to 5, so Greater Than OR Equal To (`>=`) evaluates to true.

questions.question: Why does a switch statement on an Integer require a `default` case?
options: Integers are infinite; you cannot write cases for every possible integer.
questions.explanation: Switch statements MUST be exhaustive. There's an infinite number of integers, so you must have a `default` case.
